metadata.title: Zelda: What the Series Teaches About Constraint as Design

metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: Zelda: What the Series Teaches About Con
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. A game design document begins by surfacing foundational material before any formal documentation starts. Designers must document the unhappy path first, so engineers do not discover it later in production. Characters are treated as design assets defined by mechanical and narrative function, not just backstory. If the core loop is not satisfying nothing else matters, and the loop is defined across the 30-second, 5-minute, and session scales.

artifactLines: A game design document begins by surfacing foundational material before any formal documentation starts.
Designers must document the unhappy path first, so engineers do not discover it later in production.
Characters are treated as design assets defined by mechanical and narrative function, not just backstory.
If the core loop is not satisfying nothing else matters, and the loop is defined across the 30-second, 5-minute, and session scales.

beats.narration_text: The framing that a Game Design Document forces you to document the unhappy path before engineers find it, and that if the core loop isn't satisfying nothing else matters, plus defining what the player does every 30 seconds, 5 minutes, and session, makes me want to write one. Can you (1) draft a core-loop statement for a small Zelda-like and test whether it is satisfying on its own, (2) list five unhappy-path cases I should document up front, and (3) fill in the 30-second, 5-minute, and per-session activity layers for that loop?
